flashcards.front: What are Type Wrappers in Java?
flashcards.back: Type Wrappers are classes that encapsulate primitive data types (int, char, boolean, etc.) into objects. They allow primitive values to be used in contexts that require objects, such as Collections, generics, and synchronization.

flashcards.front: List all 8 wrapper classes in Java
flashcards.back: Integer, Long, Float, Double, Short, Byte, Character, Boolean. Each wraps a primitive type: int, long, float, double, short, byte, char, boolean respectively.
Autoboxing is the automatic conversion of primitive data types to their corresponding Wrapper class objects by the Java compiler. Example: Integer i = 10; (compiler converts int 10 to Integer object)

Unboxing is the automatic conversion of Wrapper class objects back to their corresponding primitive data types by the Java compiler. Example: int x = new Integer(10); (compiler extracts primitive int from Integer object)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between parseInt() and valueOf()?
flashcards.back: parseInt() returns a primitive int value, while valueOf() returns an Integer object. Example: int x = Integer.parseInt("123"); Integer y = Integer.valueOf("123");

flashcards.front: What is the cache range for Integer wrapper class?
flashcards.back: Integer class caches Integer objects in the range -128 to 127. When using autoboxing or valueOf() within this range, the same cached object reference is returned. Outside this range, a new object is created.

flashcards.front: What is the parent class of all numeric wrapper classes?
flashcards.back: Number class (abstract) is the parent class of all numeric wrapper classes: Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double. It defines methods like byteValue(), shortValue(), intValue(), longValue(), floatValue(), doubleValue().

Wrapper classes are immutable because once an object is created, its value cannot be changed. This ensures thread safety, allows safe use in Collections, and enables caching (like Integer cache). Any operation returns a new object.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between == and equals() for Integer comparison?
flashcards.back: == compares object references (memory addresses), while equals() compares values. For Integer objects: new Integer(10) == new Integer(10) is false (different objects), but new Integer(10).equals(new Integer(10)) is true (same values). Autoboxed Integers within cache range return true for ==.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of xxxValue() methods in wrapper classes?
flashcards.back: The xxxValue() methods (like intValue(), doubleValue(), floatValue()) convert the wrapper object to different primitive types. They are defined in the Number class and overridden by each numeric wrapper class for type conversion.

flashcards.front: What are MIN_VALUE and MAX_VALUE constants in wrapper classes?
flashcards.back: These are public static final constants that represent the minimum and maximum values for each numeric wrapper type. Example: Integer.MIN_VALUE is -2147483648, Integer.MAX_VALUE is 2147483647.

flashcards.front: What happens when you compare Integer and int using ==?
flashcards.back: When comparing an Integer object with a primitive int using ==, unboxing occurs automatically. The Integer object is converted to a primitive int, so value comparison happens. Example: Integer i = 10; int j = 10; i == j evaluates to true because of unboxing.
